title: Why the benches disappeared from the station
summary: Explain how a public space changed when its benches were removed, and what that did to the people who used them.
topic: A public space that changed when seats were removed
prompt: Write 8–10 sentences explaining how a public place changed after seats or benches were taken away. Describe who used to sit there, what happened after, and how you felt. Use past simple, 'used to', and the connectors 'so' and 'after'.
hintStarters: There used to be three long benches near the entrance, but…
The people who sat there most were…
After the benches disappeared, the station felt…
I understand why they removed them, but I still think…
sampleText: There used to be three long wooden benches near the main entrance of my train station in Warsaw. People sat there for all kinds of reasons. Tired commuters rested before the climb up the stairs. Old men met their friends and talked for an hour. A woman who sold flowers sat there between trains. Sometimes, late at night, a homeless man slept on the end bench, and nobody bothered him. Then, last spring, the city removed all three benches in one morning. When I came back that evening, there were only flat metal bars on the wall, too narrow to sit on. The station looked clean, but it also looked unfriendly. After the benches disappeared, people stood near the wall or leaned against the columns. The old men stopped coming, so I do not know where they meet now. I asked a guard why the benches were gone. He said they wanted to stop people from sleeping there. I understand the reason, but the benches did more than give a place to sleep. They gave tired people a place to rest, and they made strangers sit close to each other. Now the entrance is just a space you walk through quickly. I did not think I would miss three plain benches, but I do.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: ba chiếc ghế dài bằng gỗ
những người đi làm mệt mỏi nghỉ ngơi
nói chuyện cả tiếng đồng hồ
ngủ trên chiếc ghế ở đầu hàng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the city removed all three benches
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: thành phố dỡ bỏ cả ba chiếc ghế

sampleAnnotations.phrase: flat metal bars on the wall
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: những thanh kim loại phẳng trên tường
nó cũng trông không thân thiện

sampleAnnotations.phrase: they wanted to stop people from sleeping there
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: họ muốn ngăn người ta ngủ ở đó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: I would miss three plain benches
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tôi sẽ tiếc ba chiếc ghế đơn sơ

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'Used to' for past habits and situations
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'There used to be three long wooden benches' dùng 'used to' để nói về một tình trạng có thật trong quá khứ nhưng nay không còn nữa. Cấu trúc là 'used to + động từ nguyên thể' và luôn nói về quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'There used to be three long wooden benches' uses 'used to' to talk about a situation that was true in the past but is no longer true. The structure is 'used to + base verb' and always refers to the past.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'So' to show a result
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'The old men stopped coming, so I do not know where they meet now' dùng 'so' để nối nguyên nhân với kết quả. Phần sau 'so' là hệ quả của phần trước.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The sentence 'The old men stopped coming, so I do not know where they meet now' uses 'so' to link a cause to a result. The part after 'so' is the consequence of the part before it.

sampleTranslationVi: Trước đây có ba chiếc ghế dài bằng gỗ gần lối vào chính của nhà ga ở Warsaw. Người ta ngồi đó vì đủ mọi lý do. Những người đi làm mệt mỏi nghỉ ngơi trước khi leo lên cầu thang. Những ông già gặp bạn bè và nói chuyện cả tiếng. Một người phụ nữ bán hoa ngồi đó giữa các chuyến tàu. Đôi khi, vào đêm khuya, một người vô gia cư ngủ trên chiếc ghế ở đầu hàng, và không ai làm phiền ông. Rồi mùa xuân năm ngoái, thành phố dỡ bỏ cả ba chiếc ghế chỉ trong một buổi sáng. Khi tôi quay lại buổi tối hôm đó, chỉ còn những thanh kim loại phẳng trên tường, quá hẹp để ngồi. Nhà ga trông sạch sẽ, nhưng cũng trông không thân thiện. Sau khi những chiếc ghế biến mất, người ta đứng gần tường hoặc tựa vào các cột. Những ông già ngừng đến, nên tôi không biết bây giờ họ gặp nhau ở đâu. Tôi hỏi một bảo vệ vì sao những chiếc ghế biến mất. Anh ấy nói họ muốn ngăn người ta ngủ ở đó. Tôi hiểu lý do, nhưng những chiếc ghế làm nhiều hơn là cho một chỗ để ngủ. Chúng cho người mệt mỏi một nơi để nghỉ, và khiến những người lạ ngồi gần nhau. Bây giờ lối vào chỉ là một không gian bạn đi nhanh qua. Tôi không nghĩ mình sẽ tiếc ba chiếc ghế đơn sơ, nhưng tôi tiếc.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about a past situation that no longer exists.
options: There use to be three benches near the entrance.
There used to being three benches near the entrance.
There was used to three benches near the entrance.
There used to be three benches near the entrance.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to be' is the correct form. 'Use to' drops the 'd'; 'used to being' uses the wrong verb form; 'was used to' means something different (being accustomed).
mcQuestions.hint: Which spelling is correct for past habit: 'use to' or 'used to'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the most natural sentence describing the new metal bars.
options: There were only metal bars, too narrow to sit on.
There were only metal bars, too much narrow to sit on.
There were only metal bars, so narrow for sit on.
There were only metal bars, too narrow for sitting it on.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Too narrow to sit on' uses the correct 'too + adjective + to + verb' pattern. 'Too much narrow' is wrong; 'so narrow for sit' is ungrammatical; the last adds an extra 'it'.
mcQuestions.hint: What pattern follows 'too + adjective'? Is it 'to + verb' or 'for + verb'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct sentence using 'so' to show a result.
options: The old men stopped coming, because I do not know where they meet now.
The old men stopped coming, so I do not know where they meet now.
The old men stopped to come, so I do not know where they meet now.
The old men stopped coming, so I do not know where do they meet now.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'So' correctly shows the result. 'Because' shows reason, not result; 'stopped to come' changes the meaning; the last keeps question word order in a statement.
mcQuestions.hint: Does 'so' introduce a reason or a result?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the best sentence about people standing instead of sitting.
options: People stood near the wall or leaned on against the columns.
People standed near the wall or leaned against the columns.
People stood near the wall or leaned against the columns.
People stood near the wall or leaned to the columns.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stood' and 'leaned against' are correct. 'Leaned on against' doubles prepositions; 'standed' is a wrong past form; 'leaned to' is not the right collocation.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the past tense of 'stand'? Which preposition follows 'lean'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about the city taking the benches away.
options: The city removed all three benches on one morning.
The city was removing all three benches in one morning.
The city removed all the three benches in a one morning.
The city removed all three benches in one morning.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Removed … in one morning' is correct and natural. 'On one morning' is less idiomatic; the continuous is wrong for a completed event; 'a one morning' adds an extra article.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition do we use for completing something within a period: 'in' or 'on'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best sentence explaining the guard's reason.
options: They wanted to stop people from sleeping there.
They wanted to stop people to sleep there.
They wanted stop people from sleeping there.
They wanted to stop people for sleep there.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stop someone from doing' is the correct pattern. 'To stop people to sleep' is wrong; 'wanted stop' drops 'to'; 'for sleep' uses the wrong preposition and form.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'stop someone', do we use 'from + -ing' or 'to + verb'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that best contrasts the clean look with the unfriendly feel.
options: The station looked clean, but it looked also unfriendly.
The station looked clean, but it also looked unfriendly.
The station looked cleanly, but it also looked unfriendly.
The station looked clean, but also it was looking unfriendly.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adjective 'clean' after 'looked' and natural placement of 'also' are correct. 'Looked also' is awkward; 'cleanly' is an adverb; the last misuses the continuous.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'looked', do we use an adjective or an adverb?

mcQuestions.prompt: Select the most natural sentence about the entrance now.
options: Now the entrance is just a space you walk through quick.
Now the entrance is just a space which you walk it through quickly.
Now the entrance is just a space you walk through quickly.
Now the entrance is just a space you walk quickly through it.
mcQuestions.explanation: The relative clause with adverb 'quickly' is correct. 'Quick' is an adjective; the third and fourth add an extra object 'it' that should not be there.
mcQuestions.hint: Should you add an extra 'it' after 'walk through' here?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best sentence about the benches doing more than one thing.
options: The benches did more than to give a place to sleep.
The benches did more that give a place to sleep.
The benches made more than give a place to sleep.
The benches did more than give a place to sleep.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Did more than give' is correct and natural. Option 2 adds an extra 'to'; option 3 uses 'that' instead of 'than'; option 4 uses 'made' instead of 'did'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the best closing sentence about missing the benches.
options: I did not think I would miss three plain benches, but I do.
I did not think I will miss three plain benches, but I do.
I did not thought I would miss three plain benches, but I do.
I did not think I would miss three plain benches, but I am.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would miss' after 'did not think' and the short answer 'but I do' are correct. Option 2 keeps 'will'; option 3 uses 'thought' after 'did'; option 4 uses 'am' for a non-continuous verb.
mcQuestions.hint: After a past verb like 'did not think', does the next verb shift to 'would' or stay 'will'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: When a city makes a place harder to sit or rest in, who is really being kept away? Should comfort in public spaces be for everyone, or only for some?
